Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but then I'd get distracted or feel this wave of anxiety and just... drift. I logged into the LMS a lot, maybe just to feel like I was doing something, but actual focused studying? That was harder to pin down. I'd often end up reading through materials late at night when the panic set in.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed, and then I'd cram like crazy. Other times I'd log in daily just to check things, but not really accomplish much. It really depended on how anxious I was feeling and whether I had the energy to actually focus on the content.
Q3: Usually fear, if I'm being honest. I'd get this scared feeling that I was falling behind or missing something important, so I'd log in to check. Sometimes it was just habit—like scrolling through social media, except it was the LMS. I wanted to feel connected to the course even when I wasn't really doing the work properly.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, check my phone, check the LMS. But it was more of a nervous habit than productive engagement. The peer support group I was part of helped make it feel more normal—knowing others were online too made me feel less alone in my anxiety about the course.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really helpful because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. Reading their posts made me feel less scared that I was completely lost. The written resources were good too since I learn better by reading—I could go at my own pace and highlight things without feeling rushed.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, honestly. Fifteen minutes here and there, but I found them hard to concentrate on. My mind would wander, and then I'd feel guilty for not paying attention. I'd tell myself I'd watch them later, but later never really came. The anxiety of sitting through them was too much sometimes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to assignments or exams. If it helped me feel less scared about upcoming assessments, I'd do it. But if something felt vague or optional, my brain would just categorize it as 'not urgent' and I'd push it aside indefinitely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me. Like, I knew they might help, but the anxiety of having more things on my plate was overwhelming. Required ones at least had deadlines that forced me to act. Optional stuff just floated in my mind as another thing I should be doing but wasn't, which made me feel worse.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download readings and PDFs and study from those on my tablet without being connected. I preferred highlighting and annotating offline where I could focus without notifications or the temptation to click around. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me and my notes.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd print out materials and study at my desk for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also reread my handwritten notes repeatedly before exams. The LMS would just show I'd downloaded something once, not that I'd gone over it ten times offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a reading once, download it, and then that PDF lived on my laptop for weeks while I studied from it. The LMS probably thought I glanced at it briefly, but really I was working through it slowly, paragraph by paragraph, making notes in the margins.
Q12: Midway through the semester, everything felt impossible. My anxiety spiked, and I was scared I'd already fallen too far behind to catch up. I stopped attending classes as much and just couldn't bring myself to complete assignments on time. It felt like being frozen—I wanted to engage but couldn't make myself move.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed by other things in my life, the LMS became this guilt-inducing presence. I'd log in, see all the things I hadn't done, feel terrible, and log out without accomplishing anything. The fatigue made it hard to process information even when I tried to read materials.
Q14: So many times. I'd open a lecture document or scroll through the module, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. It was like going through the motions without actually absorbing anything—just trying to feel less guilty.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly was worse than the fear of not doing them at all, somehow. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse. It's a terrible cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really made me act. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally force myself to engage, even if it was last-minute and panicked. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They were stressful but necessary for me to produce any work at all.
Q17: Feedback helped when it was encouraging, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse. I'd avoid checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get positive comments, though, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing—at least for a little while.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having so many online courses at once made everything blur together. I'd lose track of which module I was supposed to be on. The flexibility was supposed to help, but for someone like me, too much flexibility meant too many opportunities to drift away.
Q19: Clear, simple instructions made engagement easier. When things were confusing or required multiple steps to figure out, I'd get overwhelmed and give up. The peer support aspect of the course actually helped a lot—having people to check in with made the course feel less isolating and scary.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I logged in constantly because of anxiety, checking and rechecking things, but actually completing and submitting work was a different battle. The LMS activity was almost compulsive—a way to feel connected without doing the hard part of actually producing something.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Someone could study really efficiently offline and just pop into the LMS to submit work. I wish I could be that person. My high activity didn't translate to high productivity—it was more about managing my fear than actually being effective.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click, but they can't show whether you're actually learning. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the anxiety that drives meaningless clicks. They see quantity but not quality of engagement, and definitely not emotional state.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It means feeling connected to the course and your classmates, and being motivated to learn rather than just survive. For me, real engagement was rare—I was more often just trying not to drown.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much energy it took just to log in some days. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the hours I spent with downloaded materials. It definitely wouldn't show that despite everything, I actually cared about learning—I just struggled to show it in traditional ways.
